title: The Authority and Sufficiency of the Scriptures: WCF Chapter 1 (Sections 6-10)
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Authority and Sufficiency of the Scriptures - WCF Chapter 1 (sections 6-10) - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: WCF 1.6: The sufficiency of Scripture for God's glory, salvation, faith, and life
WCF 1.7-10: Perspicuity, translations, the Holy Spirit's illumination, and Scripture as the supreme judge
summary: Concluding lesson on Westminster Confession of Faith Chapter 1, covering paragraphs 6-10 alongside a parallel series on Psalm 119. Examines the sufficiency of Scripture for all things concerning God's glory, man's salvation, faith, and life; the clarity (perspicuity) of Scripture; the role of translations; the internal witness of the Holy Spirit; and Scripture as the supreme judge of all religious controversies.
transcript:
## Review and Introduction

For the adult class today you will need a Bible and a hymnal. Turn to page 848, because we are going to wrap up chapter 1 of the confession. Alongside our series on Psalm 119, we have been looking at what the whole Bible teaches about itself — which is really the first chapter of our confession, since everything else builds on that. We have looked through paragraph 5 of chapter 1. Today we will finish it, starting at paragraph 6.

## WCF 1.6: The Sufficiency of Scripture

Paragraph 6 reads:

> *The whole counsel of God, concerning all things necessary for His own glory, man's salvation, faith and life, is either expressly set down in Scripture, or by good and necessary consequence may be deduced from Scripture: unto which nothing at any time is to be added, whether by new revelations of the Spirit, or traditions of men.*

This paragraph teaches the **sufficiency of the Scriptures**. Scripture is sufficient for all things necessary for God's glory, man's salvation, what to believe, and how to live. Those are the four things Scripture is sufficient for.

The Scriptures teach this about themselves. In 2 Peter 1:2–4, the Holy Spirit through the Apostle Peter says:

> *"Grace and peace be multiplied to you in the knowledge of God and of Jesus our Lord, as His divine power has given to us all things that pertain to life and godliness."*

"All things that pertain to life and godliness" — that is, how to live and what to believe. The Apostle Paul says the same in 2 Timothy 3:16–17:

> *"All Scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness, that the man of God may be complete, thoroughly equipped for every good work."*

For everything God calls us to do, the Word of God is sufficient to equip us. There is no need for wisdom from outside the Scriptures.

### The Biggest Issue in the Church Today

In the late 1800s, early 1900s, and mid-1900s, the great battle in the Protestant church was for the inerrancy of Scripture — the fight for the perfection of God's Word, the doctrine that there are no errors in the Bible. That battle has been largely won, at least in confessional Protestant circles.

But now a more dangerous view has entered the church: the view that the Bible is not *enough*. Sure, it's good enough to get you saved — but for everything else, you need some other wisdom to know how to live. The Scriptures are not sufficient.

A few years ago a couple came to me from another church for counseling with marital issues — not complicated ones. Their pastor had told them he couldn't help them because "the Bible doesn't say anything about that particular marital issue." They came here, we looked at Scripture together, and the Scriptures addressed their struggle clearly. But this idea — that the Bible is not sufficient for modern men and women — is now widespread in the Church of Jesus Christ. That somehow for 1,900 years it was sufficient, but now we need outside wisdom to learn how to live, how to parent, how to be husbands and wives, how to rejoice in God.

And even worse, many who claim to be born-again Christians don't really view the Bible as *relevant*. "Yeah, I know what the Bible says, but my situation is unique." But Scripture says that no temptation or trial has overtaken you except what is common to man (1 Corinthians 10:13), and the Bible addresses all of it. The Bible is sufficient for everything concerning what we are to believe and everything we are called to do in this life.

### The Holy Spirit's Illumination

Paragraph 6 continues:

> *Nevertheless, we acknowledge the inward illumination of the Spirit of God to be necessary for the saving understanding of such things as are revealed in the Word.*

The Bible is sufficient — everything we need is there. Yet we need the Spirit working in us to understand what the Bible says. Apart from the Holy Spirit, the Bible cannot be properly understood unto salvation. An unbeliever cannot come and explain correctly what Paul is saying in any chapter of Romans and believe it to be true, apart from the work of the Spirit.

Paul says in 2 Corinthians that when the Jews read Moses, their eyes are veiled — but when we read Moses, our eyes are unveiled because of the Spirit of the Lord. In 1 Corinthians 2:10–12, Paul says that apart from the Spirit of God, a natural person cannot understand the things of God, because the Spirit of God reveals them. So apart from the Holy Spirit, the Bible cannot be properly understood unto salvation.

### Room for Prudence in the Church

Paragraph 6 concludes:

> *There are some circumstances concerning the worship of God and government of the Church, common to human actions and societies, which are to be ordered by the light of nature and Christian prudence, according to the general rules of the Word, which are always to be observed.*

The Bible is sufficient, yet it leaves room for the government of the church to make decisions by prudence and common sense. We believe in what is called the regulative principle of worship — the Scriptures regulate worship. We do not do anything in worship that the Bible does not command. We sing because the Bible commands it; we preach, read, pray, give, hear, eat, and bless because the Bible commands these things. We don't add to what Scripture commands — no drama, no skits — because those things are not commanded.

But the Bible does not give us every single detail. What time does worship start — 10 a.m., 11 a.m., 2 p.m.? Which songs do we sing? What instruments do we use? The order of service? These things are left to the prudence of the government of the church. And once the session decides, that is binding — not because God said 11 a.m., but because Hebrews 13:17 calls us to obey our elders.

## WCF 1.7: The Perspicuity of Scripture

Paragraph 7 deals with what is called the *perspicuity* of Scripture — a complicated word for a simple doctrine: the gospel is understandable. It can be understood by even the simplest person.

The paragraph says:

> *All things in Scripture are not alike plain in themselves, nor alike clear unto all: yet those things which are necessary to be known, believed, and observed for salvation, are so clearly propounded and opened in some place of Scripture or other, that not only the learned, but the unlearned, in the due use of the ordinary means, may attain unto a sufficient understanding of them.*

You don't need a high IQ. Even a person with limited intellectual capacity can understand the gospel through the work of the Spirit. That is a guidance for preaching as well: the preacher should never make the gospel more complicated than the Bible does, because the ordinary means of preaching is how God has appointed people to come to know the gospel.

Passages that show the gospel is clear and simple:

> *"The unfolding of your words gives light; it imparts understanding to the simple."* (Psalm 119:130)

> *"The testimony of the Lord is sure, making wise the simple."* (Psalm 19:7)

> *"From childhood you have known the Holy Scriptures, which are able to make you wise for salvation through faith which is in Christ Jesus."* (2 Timothy 3:15)

Even a child can understand the gospel of Jesus Christ. At the same time, the Bible acknowledges that some things are harder to understand. Peter says:

> *"As also in all his epistles... in which are some things hard to understand, which untaught and unstable people twist to their own destruction."* (2 Peter 3:16)

Every time I read that, I feel like Peter is giving a little dig at Paul — "his stuff is hard to understand, not like mine." But notice what Peter says: it is *untaught* and *unstable* people who twist the difficult passages. We should therefore be well-taught and stable.

## WCF 1.8: The Original Languages and Translations

Paragraph 8 says:

> *The Old Testament in Hebrew... and the New Testament in Greek... being immediately inspired by God, and by His singular care and providence kept pure in all ages, are therefore authentic... But because these original tongues are not known to all the people of God... therefore they are to be translated into the vulgar language of every nation.*

A word on "immediately": in theology, *immediately* does not mean "right away" — it means *without means*, directly. The original manuscripts in Hebrew and Greek were directly inspired by God. But they were also *preserved* for us, so we can be confident that what we have today is what the apostles and Old Testament prophets wrote.

The word "vulgar" does not mean full of profanity — it means the *common* language of the people. The Westminster divines, by subscribing to the confession, committed themselves to supporting translations into the everyday language of the people.

The Holy Spirit used common Greek (*koine* Greek) when writing the New Testament — not the elevated literary Greek of Plato and Aristotle, but the everyday language of personal letters and receipts, because God wants his Word accessible to ordinary people.

We see this principle in Nehemiah 8: Ezra read from the Hebrew, and the Levites translated into Aramaic, explaining the meaning to the people who had returned from Babylonian exile and no longer spoke Hebrew well. Right there we have a biblical sanction for translation. The New Testament authors themselves often quoted not directly from the Hebrew but from the Septuagint, a Greek translation of the Old Testament begun around 250 BC.

### On Bible Translations

Does translation philosophy matter? Yes. A paraphrase like *The Living Bible* can be helpful as a commentary, but it is not truly a translation. When it was sold as a translation, that was a problem. Similarly, the *New Living Translation* is not really a translation.

Translation philosophy runs along a spectrum. On one end is *dynamic equivalence* — translating thoughts rather than words. The NIV leans this direction. The problem is that trying to translate thoughts rather than words often results not in a translation but in an interpretation.

On the other end is a very literal word-for-word approach, like the NASB. The NASB is very precise, but its biggest weakness is that it retains archaic "thee," "thou," and "doest" specifically in prayers — as if the original languages used a different register for prayer, which they do not. For that reason I'm not its biggest fan.

The New King James Version and the ESV have very similar translation philosophies and are both on the faithful end. The King James Version is in a different category — it is no longer the "vulgar" (common) language of the people. Some say we should use it to elevate our English, but consider: the King James Version says in Judges 1 that Caleb's daughter "lighted off her donkey" — that is not an elevation of English today. And 1 Thessalonians 4:15 in the KJV says "the quick shall not prevent the dead," where "prevent" used to mean "go before" — meaning has completely shifted. The King James is a different language, not an elevated English.

## WCF 1.9: Scripture Interprets Scripture

Paragraph 9 says:

> *The infallible rule of interpretation of Scripture is the Scripture itself: and therefore, when there is a question about the true and full sense of any Scripture (which is not manifold, but one), it must be searched and known by other places that speak more clearly.*

This is the *analogy of Scripture* — we compare text with text, allowing Scripture to interpret Scripture. That is how we study the Bible.

Notice what it says: each passage of the Bible has **one meaning**, not many. Each passage may have many applications depending on your circumstances, age, culture, and station in life — but the meaning is one, independent of you. It is not "what does it mean to me" — it is "what does it mean, if I had never existed?" What is the meaning that binds everyone everywhere in every culture and time? That is the meaning.

The church helps us here. We have 2,000 years of people interpreting the Bible. We do not have to reinvent the wheel. It is actually quite prideful to think we can do a better job than all those who have gone before. We grow in knowledge, as Jesus said — "you shall know the truth" — and as the Spirit works in us, but that growth comes through faithful study, not passivity.

## WCF 1.10: Scripture Is the Supreme Judge

Paragraph 10 says:

> *The Supreme Judge, by which all controversies of religion are to be determined, and all decrees of councils, opinions of ancient writers, doctrines of men and private spirits are to be examined... can be no other but the Holy Spirit speaking in the Scripture.*

We put everything — all of life — and judge it through the lens of Scripture. All creeds, councils, opinions of ancient writers, the doctrines of men, and private spiritual impressions are to be examined and measured by Holy Scripture. Scripture is the final authority.

A note on what the confession calls "ordinary means": the ordinary means of grace in the confession are the preaching of the Word, the reading of the Word, prayer, and the sacraments. These are the means through which God saves and sanctifies his people — particularly the reading and preaching of the Word of God.
